teachingPoint: Name the completion event before designing any step. It is a permanent cross-flow contract.
positiveExample: Round 1: name 'OnboardingCompleted' before any node expansion begins
negativeExample: Round 10: emit 'UserRegistrationComplete' after all steps designed — event name reflects steps not contracts
discriminatingConstraint: The last event is the first decision — completion event names are permanent
keywords: Name completion designing step permanent cross-flow contract

teachingPoint: N independent deliveries = N separate node types. Never parameterise independent deliverables.
positiveExample: Three separate nodes C1/C2/C3 — each independently failable, independently retryable
negativeExample: One SendOnboardingMaterial node with materialType parameter — failure in one blocks all three
discriminatingConstraint: Independent deliverables = independent nodes — a materialType parameter is a loop; a loop creates a dependency
keywords: independent deliveries separate types parameterise deliverables

teachingPoint: Trace all downstream reads before writing the schema. Schema gaps discovered at NODE D are silent production failures.
positiveExample: NODE A3 schema includes onboardingMaterials: [] — NODE D reads this field 7 steps later
negativeExample: NODE A3 creates { userId, email, status } minimal record — NODE D reads absent onboardingMaterials, fails silently
discriminatingConstraint: Read contracts are satisfied at write time, not at read time
keywords: Trace downstream reads writing schema gaps discovered silent production failures

teachingPoint: When external verification is available, do not re-verify. SSO collapses the identity zone.
positiveExample: SSO path arrives at NODE B directly — Google already verified the email
negativeExample: SSO user goes through email verification as well — redundant, degrades UX
discriminatingConstraint: The verification zone is a means to proving identity. SSO provides that proof externally.
keywords: external verification available re-verify collapses identity zone

teachingPoint: The completion gate checks presence, not success. A recorded failed delivery satisfies the gate.
positiveExample: NODE D: all three materialTypes present (even all failed) → emit OnboardingCompleted
negativeExample: NODE D: requires status='delivered' for all three — transient email failure blocks user permanently
discriminatingConstraint: The gate is 'did we try' not 'did we succeed'. An absent record is a crash; a failed record is information.
keywords: completion gate checks presence success recorded failed delivery satisfies
cross-flow event name machine constant acceptance threshold completion OnboardingCompleted
teachingPoint: Cross-flow event names are MACHINE constants. Any step that emits an event consumed by another flow: acceptanceThreshold=0.95, Business arbiter mandatory, exact string literal in code.
ironRules: Cross-flow event type is a string literal — never computed or read from config
acceptanceThreshold=0.95 on all nodes emitting cross-flow events
Business arbiter mandatory on cross-flow emission nodes

keywords: token hash raw verification security breach database store event
teachingPoint: Verification tokens are stored as hashes. The raw token appears only in the event sent to the email service. A database breach exposes only hashes.
ironRules: Token stored as hash only — raw token never written to persistent storage
Raw token transmitted in the verification event only, for link construction
Breach of token index exposes only hashes — not usable tokens
codeSnippet:
storeDocument({ tokenHash: sha256(rawToken) }) // raw token NOT stored
emit({ rawToken }) // raw token in event only — for email link

keywords: intake validation uniform failure shape security information leakage field name
teachingPoint: Intake node failure shape is MACHINE (uniform). Different error shapes per field leak which validation rule fired — a security vulnerability, not just a UX issue.
ironRules: All validation failures return VALIDATION_FAILURE — same error code regardless of which field failed
No error message reveals field names, validation rules, or which check fired
codeSnippet:
return DataProcessResult.failure('VALIDATION_FAILURE', 'Input validation failed')
// Never: { field: 'email', error: 'invalid format' }

keywords: token superseded revoke audit trail status lifecycle delete expire used
teachingPoint: When replacing a token, update status to 'superseded' — never delete. Each status is distinct: expired=natural, used=submission attempt, superseded=explicitly replaced. Audit trails are non-negotiable.
ironRules: Replaced tokens marked status='superseded' with supersededAt timestamp — never deleted
Each token status encodes a distinct lifecycle event: expired, used, superseded
Token records are immutable after creation except for status transitions
codeSnippet:
await storeDocument(index, { ...token, status: 'superseded', supersededAt: now })
// Never: await deleteDocument(index, token.tokenId)

keywords: rate limit window freedom config machine enforcement resend verification tenant regional
teachingPoint: FREEDOM rate limit window, MACHINE enforcement rule. 'The window is configurable; the existence of the limit is not.' RATE_LIMIT_EXCEEDED response includes retryAfterMinutes for UI countdown.
ironRules: Rate limit window sourced from FREEDOM config — never hardcoded
Rate limit enforcement rule is MACHINE — tenants cannot disable it
codeSnippet:
const windowMin = await this.freedomConfig.get('flow01_resend_rate_limit_minutes')
// Never: const LIMIT_MINUTES = 60

keywords: tenant specific parameters freedom config machine rule social invitation content wording
teachingPoint: Social delivery nodes have tenant-specific parameters. Every such parameter is a FREEDOM config key. 'What the invitation says' is FREEDOM. 'That an invitation exists' is MACHINE.
ironRules: All tenant-specific delivery parameters sourced from FREEDOM config keys
Delivery content (wording, sender name) is FREEDOM; delivery existence is MACHINE
No hardcoded content in delivery nodes
codeSnippet:
const inviterName = await this.freedomConfig.get('flow01_invitation_inviter_name')
// Never: const inviterName = 'John from Community'
